items.title: The South Carolina Measles Outbreak Is Slowing Down
items.summary: The biggest US measles outbreak in decades may be over sooner than expected.

items.title: How the Iran War Could Jack Up Prices on Store Shelves
items.summary: The Strait of Hormuz, a busy waterway off the coast of Iran, has come to a near standstill. If the war drags on much longer, it could mean higher prices for consumer goods around the world.

items.title: Grammarly Is Facing a Class Action Lawsuit Over Its AI ‘Expert Review’ Feature
items.summary: The feature, which Grammarly shut down Wednesday, presented editing suggestions as if they came from established authors and academics—without their consent.

items.title: Ford’s new AI assistant will help fleet owners know if seatbelts are being used
items.summary: Ford rolled out an AI assistant this week that can monitor and analyze millions of data points to help its Ford Pro commercial customers boost their bottom line. The bet, and one that most other automakers are making, is that there’s money to be made in software. Ford Pro AI debuted at Work Truck Week […]

items.title: Ride hailing inDrive acquires Pakistan’s KraveMart to bolster grocery delivery
items.summary: inDrive has closed its acquisition of KraveMart, expanding grocery delivery in a market dominated by Foodpanda.

items.title: Bowen: Trump has called for an Iran uprising but the lessons from Iraq in 1991 loom large
items.summary: The US president might learn that starting wars is much easier than ending them, writes the BBC's international editor.

items.title: Countries agree to record release of emergency oil reserves as prices surge
items.summary: The G7 group of nations welcomes the idea of releasing oil in response to the surge in prices since the US-Israel war with Iran began

items.title: 'Even under missiles we carry on living' - how young Iranians are coping with war
items.summary: Iranians say they are sheltering at home and rarely venturing out on near-empty streets as the US-Israeli bombing campaign continues.

items.title: JPMorgan Chase reins in lending to private credit firms after marking down software loans
items.summary: JPMorgan's move shows that the biggest U.S. bank by assets wants to get ahead of potential turbulence involving private credit loans to software companies.

items.title: Surging oil prices could wipe out benefits from Trump's 'big beautiful bill'
items.summary: Estimates from Wall Street project that if oil prices stay elevated for too long, consumers will lose as much as they gain from the new tax law.

items.title: There's another energy market that may get hit harder than oil by Strait of Hormuz closure
items.summary: Roughly 20% of global liquefied natural gas flows through the Strait.

items.title: Stocks Sink in Broad AI Rout Sparked by China's DeepSeek
items.summary: U.S. stocks were mostly lower, with the Nasdaq leading declines as makers of AI infrastructure suffered steep falls, many in the double digits. Nvidia was down 16%.

items.title: Comex Gold, Silver Settle Lower
items.summary: Gold settled 1.4% lower, down two of the past three sessions, and silver fell 2.5%, down three of the past four sessions.

The minority members on the Senate Committee on Banking, Housing and Urban Affairs requested a congressional hearing with Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent by…

items.summary: President Trump plans to tap into the U.S.’s oil stockpile, he said Wednesday, though additional details on the release, which comes amid high prices driven by the war in Iran, were not immediately clear. Asked about the Strategic Petroleum Reserve during a visit to Ohio, Trump told TV station Local 12, “I filled it up once,…

items.title: A massive asteroid hit the North Sea and triggered a 330-foot tsunami
items.summary: A long-running debate about the Silverpit Crater beneath the North Sea has finally been resolved. Scientists now confirm it formed when a roughly 160-meter asteroid struck the seabed about 43–46 million years ago. New seismic imaging and rare shocked minerals in rock samples provided the crucial proof. The impact would have sent a massive plume skyward and unleashed a tsunami over 100 meters (330 feet) high.

items.title: Scientists may have found a pill for sleep apnea
items.summary: A European clinical trial found that the drug sulthiame significantly reduced breathing interruptions in people with moderate to severe sleep apnea. Patients taking higher doses experienced up to 47% fewer pauses in breathing and improved oxygen levels during sleep. The drug helps stabilize breathing signals in the brain, reducing airway collapse. Scientists say the findings could pave the way for a pill-based alternative to CPAP machines.

items.title: Scientists discover tiny plant trick that could supercharge crop yields
items.summary: Researchers have uncovered a molecular trick used by hornwort plants that could help future crops capture carbon dioxide more efficiently. A unique protein feature called RbcS-STAR causes the key photosynthesis enzyme Rubisco to cluster into dense compartments, helping it work more effectively. When scientists added this feature to other plants, Rubisco reorganized in the same way. The finding raises the possibility of engineering more efficient photosynthesis into major crops.

items.title: AI auto-complete may subtly shape views on social issues
items.summary: People are increasingly using AI auto-complete features when writing. Unbeknownst to them, that feature may change how they think.

items.title: One possible recipe for life on Titan is a bust
items.summary: An experiment mimicking conditions on the Saturn moon suggests that cell-like bubbles don’t form in methane lakes, puncturing hopes for alien life.

items.title: A strange ‘chirp’ in a brilliant stellar blast points to a magnetar
items.summary: Superluminous supernovas are the brightest stellar explosions in the universe. Astronomers may have found a mechanism that can trigger these events.
